name: Tutorial 5: Multi-Market Real Estate Analysis
description: Demonstrates a reusable market analysis workflow. Phase 1 analyzes Austin listings — pricing by neighborhood, property type benchmarking, outlier detection, and neighborhood tier performance via reference table JOIN. Phase 2 saves the workflow, swaps the dataset to Denver via the Edit panel, and reruns the entire analysis. The Edit Workflow feature is the payoff: same methodology, new market, two minutes.

narration.pre: A real estate advisory firm evaluates residential markets for investment clients. Their analysts run the same market analysis on every new city. We start with Austin — 2,000 listings across 10 neighborhoods.
narration.post: Session initialized. Phase 1 analyzes Austin using Ask Your Data for natural-language queries, Schema inspection, and a reference table JOIN. Phase 2 saves the workflow, swaps to Denver via the Edit panel, and reruns — demonstrating how a recorded analysis becomes a reusable template.

narration.pre: Importing Austin residential listings. 2,000 rows, 14 columns — LISTING_ID through AGENT_ID. Every listing has price, square footage, days on market, neighborhood, and status (Sold or Active).
narration.post: Austin data loaded. The summary cards at the top now show 2,000 rows, 14 columns, and the file source. Before we start querying, let's inspect the schema to see exactly what we're working with.

narration.pre: Click Schema to see every column name and data type in the dataset. This is the first thing an analyst should do after import — understand the structure before asking questions.
narration.post: 14 columns displayed. LIST_PRICE and SALE_PRICE are numeric. NEIGHBORHOOD and PROPERTY_TYPE are categorical — good candidates for GROUP BY. DAYS_ON_MARKET is our velocity metric. ZIP_CODE is the join key we will use later to JOIN against a reference table. Now let's see what the AI finds interesting about this data.

narration.pre: Switch to the Insights view. The AI reads the column names and statistics — never the actual row data — and generates 3-5 analytical observations automatically. This happens the moment you import a dataset. No prompting required.
narration.post: The AI flagged price concentration — a small number of listings pull the average well above the median. It also identified days-on-market variation across neighborhoods and the sold/active split. Each insight card has an Explore in Query button that loads the supporting SQL. These are starting points, not final answers. Now let's load a reference table and start querying.

narration.pre: Now we load a reference table. Reference tables are small lookup files that enrich your dataset through SQL JOINs. This one classifies each ZIP code into a market tier — Tier 1 for premium, Tier 2 for mid-market, Tier 3 for entry-level. Click + Reference Table in the sidebar to import it.
narration.post: Reference table loaded: 20 rows, 5 columns — zip_code, city, neighborhood, tier, notes. It appears in the sidebar with a REF badge. In SQL, use the table name 'neighborhood_tier_map' in your JOIN clause. The join key is ZIP_CODE in the listings matched against zip_code in the reference table. We will use this in the last Austin query.

details.question: What is the average list price and average days on market by neighborhood?

narration.pre: Type a question in plain English into Ask Your Data: 'What is the average list price and average days on market by neighborhood?' The AI reads the column names and types — it never sees your actual data — and writes the SQL for you.
narration.post: SQL generated and executed. Tarrytown leads at $830K average. Southeast Austin has the highest days on market at 35 — nearly double Downtown. The AI picked the right columns, the right aggregation, and the right GROUP BY — all from a plain English question.

details.question: What is the average price per square foot by property type?

narration.pre: Another plain-English question: 'What is the average price per square foot by property type?' The AI uses the PRICE_PER_SQFT column it saw in the schema.
narration.post: 4 property types. Townhouses lead on price per sqft. Condos and single family homes are close behind — the downtown high-rise condo stock pulls the condo average up. Two questions, two results, zero SQL written by hand.

details.sql:
WITH neighborhood_avg AS (
    SELECT NEIGHBORHOOD, AVG(LIST_PRICE) AS avg_price
    FROM dataset
    GROUP BY NEIGHBORHOOD
)
SELECT
    d.LISTING_ID,
    d.NEIGHBORHOOD,
    d.PROPERTY_TYPE,
    d.LIST_PRICE,
    ROUND(na.avg_price, 0) AS neighborhood_avg,
    ROUND(100.0 * (d.LIST_PRICE - na.avg_price) / na.avg_price, 1) AS pct_above_avg
FROM dataset d
JOIN neighborhood_avg na ON d.NEIGHBORHOOD = na.NEIGHBORHOOD
WHERE d.LIST_PRICE > na.avg_price * 1.8
ORDER BY d.LIST_PRICE DESC

narration.pre: Outlier detection: listings priced more than 80% above the average list price for their neighborhood. This uses a CTE subquery — more complex than what we'd type in plain English, so we use the SQL editor directly. The query calculates each neighborhood's average, then filters to listings 80% above it.
narration.post: 36 outlier listings found. Downtown and Tarrytown dominate — luxury construction pulling well above neighborhood averages. The top listing is nearly $2M in Downtown against a $660K neighborhood average. Each of these needs manual review before it enters a valuation model.

details.sql:
SELECT
    t.tier,
    d.STATUS,
    COUNT(*) AS listing_count,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (PARTITION BY t.tier), 1) AS pct
FROM dataset d
JOIN neighborhood_tier_map t ON d.ZIP_CODE = t.zip_code
GROUP BY t.tier, d.STATUS
ORDER BY t.tier, d.STATUS

narration.pre: This is the JOIN step — the key capability that reference tables unlock. Look at the SQL: FROM dataset d JOIN neighborhood_tier_map t ON d.ZIP_CODE = t.zip_code. The listings table has ZIP_CODE. The reference table has zip_code and tier. The JOIN connects them, so we can GROUP BY tier without ever adding a tier column to the original data. The window function OVER (PARTITION BY t.tier) calculates percentages within each tier.
narration.post: 6 rows — 2 per tier (Sold and Active). Tier 1 neighborhoods show 63% sold, the fastest clearance rate. Without the reference table, you would need to manually classify 10 neighborhoods into tiers before running this query. The JOIN does it in one step. This is why reference tables matter: they let you enrich and segment your data without modifying the original file.

narration.pre: The Austin analysis is complete. Before moving to the next market, we save this as a stored workflow. Every query, every reference table, every step — saved and replayable.
narration.post: Workflow saved as 'Market Analysis — Austin 2024'. Now watch what happens when a new market comes in.

narration.pre: A new client wants a Denver market analysis. Same investment thesis, same methodology. Instead of starting from scratch, we open the Edit panel on the Austin workflow.
narration.post: This is the Edit panel. The original Austin file is on the left — that is what the workflow was recorded with. The current mapping is on the right — that is what it will use when we run it. We are going to swap the right side from Austin to Denver. The reference table stays — the neighborhood tier map applies to both cities.

narration.pre: Click Choose File next to the Austin dataset. Select the Denver listings file.
narration.post: File selected. The current mapping now shows denver_listings.csv with 1,800 rows. The system is checking whether Denver's columns match what the workflow expects.

Green across the board. All 14 columns match. The workflow was built on Austin data — but Denver uses the exact same schema. Every query will run without a single modification. This is what a consistent data standard buys you. Run Now is active.

narration.pre: Click Run Now. Same queries. Denver data. Watch the results change.
narration.post: The Edit panel closes. Every step from the Austin workflow now replays against the Denver dataset.

narration.pre: Denver data imported. The same queries now run against 1,800 Denver listings.
narration.post: Denver loaded: 1,800 rows, 14 columns. Same schema as Austin. The queries will execute identically — only the data changes.

details.sql:
SELECT
    NEIGHBORHOOD,
    ROUND(AVG(LIST_PRICE), 0) AS avg_list_price,
    ROUND(AVG(DAYS_ON_MARKET), 1) AS avg_days_on_market
FROM dataset
GROUP BY NEIGHBORHOOD
ORDER BY avg_list_price DESC

narration.pre: Same query, Denver data. Average list price and days on market by neighborhood.
narration.post: Cherry Creek at $928K average — above Austin's most expensive neighborhood. Washington Park moves in under 11 days — half Austin's pace. Denver is a faster, more expensive market across the board.

details.sql:
WITH neighborhood_avg AS (
    SELECT NEIGHBORHOOD, AVG(LIST_PRICE) AS avg_price
    FROM dataset
    GROUP BY NEIGHBORHOOD
)
SELECT
    d.LISTING_ID,
    d.NEIGHBORHOOD,
    d.PROPERTY_TYPE,
    d.LIST_PRICE,
    ROUND(na.avg_price, 0) AS neighborhood_avg,
    ROUND(100.0 * (d.LIST_PRICE - na.avg_price) / na.avg_price, 1) AS pct_above_avg
FROM dataset d
JOIN neighborhood_avg na ON d.NEIGHBORHOOD = na.NEIGHBORHOOD
WHERE d.LIST_PRICE > na.avg_price * 1.8
ORDER BY d.LIST_PRICE DESC

narration.pre: Outlier detection on Denver — same 80% above neighborhood average threshold.
narration.post: 42 outlier listings in Denver — more than Austin's 36. Concentrated in Cherry Creek and Washington Park — luxury construction and estate properties pulling the average up. Higher outlier count reflects Denver's wider price dispersion in premium neighborhoods.

narration.pre: Open the Session Log. Both analyses — Austin and Denver — are documented with timestamps, SQL, and row counts.
narration.post: Two markets. Two stored workflows. Next market takes two minutes — open Edit, swap the file, run. The methodology is locked. Only the data changes. Every query from both analyses is documented. Methodology is fully auditable.

resume_state.last_sql:
WITH neighborhood_avg AS (
    SELECT NEIGHBORHOOD, AVG(LIST_PRICE) AS avg_price
    FROM dataset
    GROUP BY NEIGHBORHOOD
)
SELECT
    d.LISTING_ID,
    d.NEIGHBORHOOD,
    d.PROPERTY_TYPE,
    d.LIST_PRICE,
    ROUND(na.avg_price, 0) AS neighborhood_avg,
    ROUND(100.0 * (d.LIST_PRICE - na.avg_price) / na.avg_price, 1) AS pct_above_avg
FROM dataset d
JOIN neighborhood_avg na ON d.NEIGHBORHOOD = na.NEIGHBORHOOD
WHERE d.LIST_PRICE > na.avg_price * 1.8
ORDER BY d.LIST_PRICE DESC
